articles.title: The Mind in the Tentacles
paragraphs: For most of scientific history, intelligence was {thought} to be the {special} property of creatures with backbones. Mammals, birds, and even some reptiles seemed to share a basic kind of {cleverness}, while everything else was assumed to run on instinct. The octopus has {quietly} {broken} that idea.

Octopuses are mollusks — closer relatives of clams than of any thinking animal we know. They have no skeleton, no central seat of consciousness in the way humans do, and a lifespan that rarely passes two years. By any reasonable {rule} of biology, they should not be smart. Yet they {solve} puzzles, open jars, escape from sealed tanks, and recognize individual human faces. They also seem to play, a behavior once thought to belong only to mammals and a few {clever} birds.

The {strangest} part of octopus intelligence is where it lives. Roughly two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are not in its brain but in its arms. Each arm can taste, touch, and decide on its own, then report back to the central brain. In a real sense, an octopus is a creature {thinking} in eight directions at once.

Researchers studying octopuses often describe a feeling that the animal is {studying} them back. Aquarium keepers report octopuses spraying water at workers they dislike, {hiding} food from cameras, and waiting for the lights to dim before trying to escape. These are not the actions of an instinct-driven machine.
The octopus reminds us that intelligence is not one thing. It is a {strategy} life has invented more than once, in forms we are only beginning to {understand}.

quizGroups.sentence: For most of scientific history, intelligence was {thought} to be the {special} property of creatures with backbones.

quizGroups.sentence: The {strangest} part of octopus intelligence is where it lives.

articles.title: The Memory of Crows
paragraphs: Crows are perhaps the most {underrated} thinkers in the animal world. They live near humans, blend into the background, and seem ordinary — yet behind those black eyes is a brain that {rivals} that of a chimpanzee.

Researchers in Seattle once {documented} a remarkable behavior. Volunteers wore strange masks while capturing and banding wild crows. The crows did not forget. Years later, those same volunteers were still being scolded and dive-bombed wherever they walked, even when they wore ordinary clothes. The masks alone were enough.

Crows can {fashion} tools out of twigs and leaves. They drop nuts onto roads and wait for cars to crack them. In one famous experiment, a crow named Betty bent a straight wire into a hook to retrieve food from a tube — a feat once thought {impossible} for any non-human animal.

What makes crows {unusual} is not just their cleverness, but their social memory. They {remember} faces, both kind and threatening, and they teach what they have learned to younger crows. A grudge held by one bird can outlast the lifetime of the human who offended it.

When a crow dies, the others sometimes gather. They circle the body {silently}, dropping sticks and stones near it before flying away. Scientists are not sure what these gatherings mean — perhaps a kind of {investigation}, perhaps something closer to grief. The crow forces us to {rethink} what intelligence looks like.

quizGroups.sentence: Crows are perhaps the most {underrated} thinkers in the animal world.

quizGroups.sentence: Behind those black eyes is a brain that {rivals} that of a chimpanzee.
Researchers in Seattle once {documented} a remarkable behavior.

quizGroups.correct: to make or shape something by hand

A feat once thought {impossible} for any non-human animal.
What makes crows {unusual} is not just their cleverness, but their social memory.

quizGroups.correct: to keep in mind from the past
quizGroups.sentence: They {remember} faces, both kind and threatening.

quizGroups.correct: to think about again from a new angle

articles.title: The Long Goodbye
paragraphs: In 1977, NASA {launched} two small probes called Voyager 1 and Voyager 2. They were never meant to leave the solar system. Their {initial} mission was a quick tour of Jupiter and Saturn — five years, maybe six, then silence.

Almost fifty years later, both spacecraft are still {transmitting} signals. Voyager 1 is now over twenty-three billion kilometers from Earth, the farthest human-made object that has ever existed. Every signal it sends takes more than twenty-two hours to reach us.

Bolted to each probe is a thin gold disk known as the Golden Record. It contains greetings in fifty-five languages, the cry of a baby, music from every continent, and instructions for any intelligent being who might one day find it. The {chance} of that happening is {minuscule}. The {gesture} is nonetheless deeply human.

Both probes have now {entered} interstellar space, the region beyond the sun's solar wind. Their power is fading. Engineers shut off one instrument at a time to {preserve} the last drops of energy from their plutonium hearts. By the early 2030s, the probes will go silent.

It is {strange} to think of two small machines, no bigger than a small car, still racing into the dark — carrying our hopes with them, {quietly}, like a message in a bottle thrown into a sea no one has ever crossed.

quizGroups.sentence: In 1977, NASA {launched} two small probes called Voyager 1 and Voyager 2.
Their {initial} mission was a quick tour of Jupiter and Saturn.

The {chance} of that happening is {minuscule}.
The {gesture} is nonetheless deeply human.

quizGroups.sentence: Engineers shut off one instrument at a time to {preserve} the last drops of energy from their plutonium hearts.

articles.title: The Library That Burned Twice
paragraphs: The Library of Alexandria is the most {famous} library that no one alive has ever seen. Founded in Egypt around 280 BCE, it was the heart of an ambitious project: to collect, in one place, every written work known to the ancient world.

Its scholars {gathered} scrolls from across the Mediterranean. Ships docking in Alexandria were searched not for treasure but for books, which were copied, the originals kept, and the copies returned. At its height, the library may have held over half a million scrolls — works of mathematics, medicine, poetry, history, and astronomy now {lost} forever.

We do not know exactly how it ended. The library was damaged during Julius Caesar's siege of the city in 48 BCE, when fire spread from the harbor into nearby buildings. Later rulers {tried} to rebuild what had burned. Centuries of unrest, religious conflict, and the {arrival} of new powers slowly chipped away at what remained.

By the seventh century, the library was effectively gone. The exact {loss} cannot be measured. Many works survived only as titles quoted by other writers — the rest of the text {destroyed} long before printing existed to copy it.

What disappeared at Alexandria did not vanish all at once. It faded {slowly}, in pieces, the way most great things end. The story still {haunts} historians, who wonder how much of the ancient world's knowledge might have been {preserved} if even one of those fires had been put out in time.

The exact {loss} cannot be measured.

quizGroups.correct: stays in the mind in a troubling way
quizGroups.sentence: The story still {haunts} historians.

quizGroups.correct: kept safe from harm or loss
quizGroups.sentence: Historians wonder how much of the ancient world's knowledge might have been {preserved} if even one of those fires had been put out in time.

articles.title: Twice the Prize
paragraphs: Marie Curie's life was {remarkable} for many reasons, but one detail still surprises people: she remains the only person ever to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences — physics and chemistry. The first came in 1903, the second in 1911.

Born in Poland in 1867, she could not study at her own country's universities, which were closed to women at the time. {Stubborn} and brilliant, she moved to Paris, slept in unheated attics, and lived on bread and tea so she could attend the Sorbonne — one of the few European universities not {barred} to her.

Working with her husband Pierre in a leaking shed near the school, she {carefully} processed tons of pitchblende ore by hand. Out of it she {discovered} two new elements: polonium, named after her homeland, and radium, which glowed faintly in the dark.

{Praise} for the work was real but uneven. When she won her first Nobel, the committee tried to hand the prize only to her husband and another colleague. Pierre refused unless Marie's name was added. She {kept} working long after his death in 1906.

What no one yet understood was the danger she had been studying. Years of {exposure} to radiation slowly ruined her health. She worked {tirelessly} until her death in 1934. Her notebooks are still radioactive a century later — quietly outshining and {overshadowed} by the bigger story they contain.

quizGroups.sentence: Marie Curie's life was {remarkable} for many reasons.
{Stubborn} and brilliant, she moved to Paris.
One of the few European universities not {barred} to her.
She {carefully} processed tons of pitchblende ore by hand.

quizGroups.sentence: Out of it she {discovered} two new elements: polonium and radium.

articles.title: The Watchers
paragraphs: In 1964, news reports claimed that thirty-eight neighbors had watched a young woman attacked outside her New York apartment without any of them calling for help. Many details were later shown to be wrong. But a generation of psychologists had already been {puzzled} by a question the story raised: why do people sometimes watch and do nothing while a stranger needs help?

Earlier studies had shown that small groups acted faster in emergencies than large ones. The result was strange. A single witness usually called for help right away. Larger groups often {ignored} the same situation, or responded so {quickly} after long pauses that help came too late.

Two researchers, Bibb Latané and John Darley, {studied} this carefully through staged experiments. In one, smoke poured into a waiting room. Participants alone were quick to {react}. Participants in groups, even small ones, often sat still — glancing around, waiting for someone else to move first.

They called the pattern the bystander effect. It is more {common} than people like to admit. The {cause} is not cruelty but a kind of social paralysis. Responsibility seems {spread} so thinly across a crowd that no single person feels truly responsible.

Knowing about the effect changes things. Witnesses who have read about it act more often. The lesson is simple but {uneasy}: in a crowd, look directly at someone, point, and ask them by description. Doing so cuts through the silence — and reminds everyone, including yourself, that helping is {deeply} a choice.

quizGroups.sentence: Psychologists had been {puzzled} by a question the story raised.
Larger groups often {ignored} the same situation.

quizGroups.sentence: Or responded so {quickly} after long pauses that help came too late.
Two researchers, Bibb Latané and John Darley, {studied} this carefully through staged experiments.

quizGroups.correct: to act in response to something
quizGroups.sentence: Participants alone were quick to {react}.
It is more {common} than people like to admit.
The {cause} is not cruelty but a kind of social paralysis.
Responsibility seems {spread} so thinly across a crowd that no single person feels truly responsible.

articles.title: The Map in Your Pocket
paragraphs: Most people use GPS several times a day without giving it a second thought. The voice from a phone telling a driver to turn right has become so {ordinary} that the system behind it feels almost natural — as if the world had always known where everyone was.

It hadn't. GPS began as a Cold War project, {designed} in the 1970s for the U.S. military. Its purpose was to help submarines and missiles know their position to within a few meters. The system uses signals from a constellation of satellites, each carrying a clock so precise that it can measure time differences smaller than a millionth of a second. From those tiny gaps, your phone can {track} your location in real time.

In 1983, a civilian airliner was shot down after wandering off course over Soviet airspace. President Reagan {released} GPS to civilian use partly in response. For years the public version was deliberately less accurate than the military version. That throttling was finally lifted in 2000, and the system rapidly {expanded} into everything from delivery apps to farm equipment.

What feels magical is mostly geometry. By comparing the {tiny} delays in signals from at least four satellites, your phone solves a few equations and locates itself on Earth. We {rely} on this so heavily that disruption can paralyze entire cities — flights, banking, and shipping all use it.

The system is also {vulnerable}. Cheap jammers and weather in space can blank it out for hours. It is {remarkably} fragile for something we trust so much, working {invisibly} above us in a thin shell of orbiting clocks no one ever sees.

GPS began as a Cold War project, {designed} in the 1970s for the U.S. military.
From those tiny gaps, your phone can {track} your location in real time.

The system rapidly {expanded} into everything from delivery apps to farm equipment.
By comparing the {tiny} delays in signals from at least four satellites.

articles.title: When the Reef Turns White
paragraphs: A healthy coral reef is one of the most {vibrant} places on Earth. Bands of orange, blue, and green ripple across the seafloor as fish, eels, and sea turtles weave through structures that took thousands of years to grow. Beneath that color, however, is a {fragile} partnership.

Coral itself is a small, simple animal. Its color comes from algae living inside its tissues — tiny green cells that photosynthesize sunlight and pass food back to the coral. In return, the coral provides {shelter}. The two have lived together for hundreds of millions of years.

When the surrounding water {rises} just a few degrees above normal, the partnership breaks. Stressed corals {expel} the algae from their bodies. Without their tenants, the corals lose their color and turn ghostly white — what scientists call coral bleaching. They do not die immediately, but without the algae they slowly {starve}.

Bleaching events are now happening with frightening regularity. The Great Barrier Reef has bleached five times since 2016. Each event is a {warning} that the ocean is heating {rapidly}, and the chemistry of seawater is changing along with it.

Some reefs do {recover} when temperatures fall. But each event leaves a smaller, weaker reef behind. Marine biologists describe walking through bleached areas as a strange kind of grief — the same shapes that once swarmed with life, now silent and {fading}, like a forest after a fire that no one saw.

articles.title: The Sleeping Suites
paragraphs: Johann Sebastian Bach wrote his Six Suites for Unaccompanied Cello around 1720. For nearly two hundred years they sat almost {neglected}, treated as exercises rather than music. Cellists used them to warm up; concert audiences rarely heard them.

In 1890, a thirteen-year-old boy named Pablo Casals {stumbled} on a tattered copy of the suites in a secondhand music shop in Barcelona. He could not afford it at first. He went back, bought the score, and for the next twelve years played them every morning before he would let himself perform them in public. He felt the {ancient} pages contained something no one had yet heard.

Casals {studied} each note as if reading a private letter from a man dead for over a hundred years. When he finally {performed} the full set in 1936, the music world reacted as if the pieces had been written yesterday.

The recordings he made shortly after {transformed} how cellists understood Bach. The suites are now considered the heart of the cello repertoire — {intimate} works that sound conversational and {deceptively} simple, then turn, in a single phrase, into something much vaster.

Casals's quiet {reverence} for the music still {lingers} over performances today. Every cellist who plays the suites carries, somewhere in the bow arm, the memory of a thirteen-year-old reaching for a forgotten book on a dusty shelf — and the long silence that came before.

quizGroups.sentence: For nearly two hundred years they sat almost {neglected}.

articles.title: The Last Train Home
paragraphs: She boarded the train just before midnight, {weary} from a day that had asked too much of her. The car was nearly {deserted}. A single elderly man dozed near the back, his newspaper folded across his chest like a paper blanket.

She took the seat by the window. The platform outside was wet from an earlier rain. She {noticed} her own reflection in the glass before the train began to move — pale, eyes too dark, hair pulled back in a way she did not remember doing.

The conductor came and went. The train pulled out of the station and the city's lights began to {linger}, then thin, then disappear entirely. She {wondered} how many times she had taken this same route home, and whether anyone, looking out from any of the apartment windows above, had ever noticed her at all.

She held her bag {tightly} in her lap. She let her forehead rest against the cold window and listened to the rails {softly} clicking beneath her — a small repeating sound that had carried her, she realized, through every difficult {departure} of her adult life.

Somewhere outside, a yard light flickered and was gone. She closed her eyes. Memory, she had read once, is the {fragile} part of the self — the first to leave and the last to return. Tonight her memory {ached}, the way a tooth aches before it finally lets go.

articles.title: The Mile in Four
paragraphs: For most of the twentieth century, running a mile in under four minutes was thought to be a {barrier} that no human body could cross. Doctors warned that the heart and lungs would not survive the strain. Coaches were {convinced} the human frame had reached its limits. Many believed it was simply impossible.

Roger Bannister, a young British medical student, did not agree. He {trained} on a public track between hospital shifts, often in heavy boots to make daily runs feel harder. He ran calculations about pace and pulse the way another doctor might run blood tests.

On a {blustery} afternoon in May 1954, in front of a small crowd in Oxford, he stepped onto the track with two friends who had agreed to set the pace for him. The wind almost made him quit before the start. He ran anyway. When he crossed the line, the announcer's voice cracked over the loudspeaker. The official time, he said, was three minutes and — the rest was lost in the cheering. Bannister had {shattered} the four-minute mile.

What followed was odd. Within forty-six days, another runner broke the same barrier. Within three years, sixteen runners had. The wall, it turned out, had been more in the head than in the body. Once Bannister proved it could be done, others {stunned} themselves by doing it too. The barrier had been mental, and they could finally {grasp} that.

Records have fallen {rapidly} since. The mile is now run nearly seventeen seconds faster. But Bannister's run, on an {ordinary} cinder track in front of a few hundred people, still {lingers} as a small lesson — that what looks like the edge of human possibility is sometimes only the edge of what we have agreed to believe.

quizGroups.sentence: Running a mile in under four minutes was thought to be a {barrier} that no human body could cross.
Coaches were {convinced} the human frame had reached its limits.

Bannister had {shattered} the four-minute mile.

The barrier had been mental, and they could finally {grasp} that.

quizGroups.sentence: Bannister's run still {lingers} as a small lesson.
